demographics: I am 59 years old. I am male. I am Black or African American. I am married. I had 1 year of college education. My family income is $12,500 to $14,999. I am an independent (neither Democrat nor Republican). I am moderate (middle of the road). I never attend religious services.

events.event_text: I witnessed a police officer using excessive force during a protest when I was 32. It shook my belief in how fairly our justice system treats everyone.
I lost my job at the factory of 15 years due to automated changes. I was 45 and had a family to support. It was a hard time, but I found a new job after a year.

I was denied a promotion at work after my white colleague, who started after me, got it instead. The supervisor said I 'wasn't a good fit for the position'. I couldn't help but feel it was because of my race. It made me question the fairness of the system I've been working in.

When I was 38, I decided to join the local Neighborhood Watch group after there had been a series of robberies in our area. I thought it was important to take a proactive role in keeping our community safe.

At 38, I was pulled over by the police on my way home from work. They assumed I was driving under the influence androughly searched me, even though I cooperated and had nothing on me. It turned out they were wrong and I wasn't charged, but the incident left me feeling humiliated and unfairly targeted.

At 42, my best friend of 25 years, who is white, was promoted to manager at our company despite having less experience and qualifications than me. When I questioned the decision, I was accused of being combatant and eventually fired a month later. I feel betrayed and discriminated against.

At 52, I decided to run for city council as an independent candidate to advocate for changes in our local police department after a series of high-profile misconduct cases. I knew it would be challenging, but I felt it was my civic duty to try and make a difference.

compressed_summaries.summary: This person, at 32, witnessed police brutality during a protest, challenging their belief in the justice system's fairness. At 45, they lost their long-term job due to automation, but found new employment after a year. They experienced racial discrimination at work, being denied a promotion in favor of a less qualified white colleague.

This person, at 38, joined the Neighborhood Watch after robberies, but was later pulled over and humiliated by police despite cooperating. At 42, they were fired after questioning a friend's promotion, feeling betrayed and discriminated against. At 52, they ran for city council to advocate for police reform.

persona_card: "Meet a 59-year-old Black male, married, with a moderate, independent political stance, who is not religiously affiliated. Despite a year of college and a family income of $12,500 - $14,999, he leans towards economic redistribution, believing in a more active government role. He holds strong structuralist views on racial inequality, having witnessed and experienced discrimination firsthand, yet maintains a nuanced, open attitude towards other social outgroups. His trust in institutions, particularly the legal system, is low due to personal experiences with police brutality and racial bias."
